questions.objective_ref: LO1 — Define an AI agent in plain language and explain how it differs from a standalone LLM.
questions.stem_summary: What makes an AI agent different from a standalone LLM?

questions.objective_ref: LO2 — Identify and describe the four core components: LLM core, memory, tools, and the planning loop.
questions.stem_summary: Which statement about the four-component list is correct?

questions.objective_ref: LO3 — Explain the observe-think-act cycle and trace how a simple task moves through it step by step.
questions.stem_summary: Arrange the first three phases of the observe-think-act cycle for a concrete task.

questions.objective_ref: LO4 — Distinguish short-term memory from long-term memory; give an example of each.
questions.stem_summary: Which memory type matches cross-session preference recall vs. within-task step tracking?

questions.objective_ref: LO5 — Give two examples of tools an agent might use and explain what a tool enables that an LLM alone cannot do.
questions.stem_summary: What does a tool enable an agent to do that a standalone LLM cannot?

questions.objective_ref: LO6 — Recognise what an agentic workflow is; identify two real-world systems that rely on one.
questions.stem_summary: Why is an autonomous multi-step customer service system correctly called an agentic workflow?

questions.objective_ref: LO3 — Explain the observe-think-act cycle and trace how a simple task moves through it step by step.
questions.stem_summary: Describe the observe-think-act cycle: name each phase, explain it, and describe the stopping condition.
